proposed_short: The compound conditional (condizionale passato): the conditional of the auxiliary plus a past participle, as avrei finito 'I would have finished'.
proposed_long: The conditional perfect, called the condizionale passato in Italian, expresses what would have happened: 'Sarei venuto se avessi potuto' (I would have come if I had been able to). It is built from the conditional of the auxiliary (avere or essere) plus the past participle, mirroring the passato prossimo but with the auxiliary in the conditional: avrei finito, saresti partito, saremmo arrivati. The same auxiliary-choice and participle-agreement rules apply, so motion and change-of-state verbs take essere and agree the participle with the subject (sarebbe andata), while most others take avere with an invariable participle (avrei finito). Italian also uses it where English uses 'would' in reported speech about the future: 'Ha detto che sarebbe venuto' (he said he would come), the reported future-in-the-past.
rationale: Used throughout the condizionale_passato explanations. The existing 'conditional' glossary entry mentions the past conditional in one line, but there is no dedicated entry for the compound the way there is 'compound tense', 'passato prossimo', and 'trapassato prossimo' for their compounds; this parallels the 'future perfect' (futuro anteriore) entry FutureFormationAuthor proposed for the sibling tree. NOTE on terms I did NOT re-propose to avoid duplicates: the conditional explanations lean on 'theme vowel', 'syncopation', and 'assimilation' (all proposed in glossary_suggestions_verb_form.future.json, still pending merge) and 'suppletive' (proposed in glossary_suggestions_verb_form.present_indicative.json, still pending). The conditional shares these exactly with the future, so I rely on the future/present proposals rather than restating them here.
